title: Laundry App
summary: Manage intake workflows, dynamic pricing models, vendor invoicing, P&L reporting, and granular audit trails across Men's, Ladies', and Furniture cleaning branches from a single unified control center.
environment: Branch workflow coverage for Men's, Ladies', and Furniture cleaning
Role-focused control center for intake, pricing, invoicing, reporting, and audit review
Dynamic pricing rules for service categories, item types, quantities, and branch-specific operations
objectives: Replace scattered manual tracking with a single operational dashboard for laundry branch activity
Create a structured intake flow that records customer orders, service categories, item details, and pricing decisions
Support dynamic pricing so different cleaning branches and item types can be priced without changing code
Generate vendor invoices and operational financial summaries from the same source of truth
Provide P&L visibility and audit trails so managers can review changes, totals, and branch performance

steps.title: Mapped the laundry operations model
steps.description: Separated the business into Men's, Ladies', and Furniture cleaning branches, then modelled the key records each branch needs: customer intake, services, item quantities, pricing, vendors, invoices, and financial totals.

steps.title: Built the intake workflow
steps.description: Created a guided flow for capturing orders with enough structure to reduce missed details while still keeping daily counter work fast. The goal was to make order entry consistent before reporting and invoicing ever happen.

steps.title: Added dynamic pricing controls
steps.description: Moved pricing behavior into configurable rules so branch-specific service pricing, item categories, and quantity-based totals can be managed from the application instead of being hard-coded.

steps.title: Connected vendor invoicing and financial reports
steps.description: Used the captured operational data to drive vendor invoice preparation and P&L summaries. This keeps invoices, revenue, cost tracking, and branch-level performance tied to the same records.

steps.title: Implemented audit-friendly tracking
steps.description: Added granular audit trail coverage so important operational and financial changes can be reviewed later. For a business workflow app, this is as important as the main forms because it supports accountability.

steps.title: Deployed and reviewed as a production-style lab
steps.description: Published the app on Vercel and kept the lab focused on business workflow design, data modelling, reporting accuracy, and operational usability rather than only the frontend screens.

outcomes: Working cloud deployment available for review at https://firoznep-laundry-app.vercel.app/
Converted a business process into a structured web application with intake, pricing, invoices, P&L, and audit history
Demonstrates practical full-stack thinking: workflow design, data relationships, reporting, deployment, and user operations
Useful portfolio lab because it shows how technical work can solve a real branch-management problem, not only display static data
Recommended next polish: add screenshots, document user roles, show sample reports, capture test cases for pricing rules, and describe backup/export strategy

title: PassDesk Password Manager
summary: Built and deployed a cloud-hosted password manager with a zero-knowledge-style security model: vault encryption keys are derived in the browser, encrypted records are stored in Neon, and recovery requires both registered email access and the recovery code.
environment: Frontend and API hosted on Vercel
Neon PostgreSQL database for encrypted vault metadata and records
Client-side key derivation and encryption before data leaves the browser
Email-backed recovery workflow tied to a user-held recovery code
objectives: Create a working password keeper where plaintext passwords are never stored in the database
Derive encryption keys client-side from the user's master password instead of keeping vault keys on the server
Make account recovery intentionally strict: recovery is possible only with real email ownership and the recovery code
Document the unrecoverable state clearly so users understand that losing both the master password and recovery code means vault data cannot be decrypted
Deploy the full stack on cloud services and verify the production flow end to end

steps.title: Defined the trust boundary
steps.description: Kept the server responsible for authentication, storage, and delivery while keeping vault encryption and decryption in the browser. This makes Neon a store for encrypted records, not a place where usable passwords are available.

steps.title: Implemented master-password based vault access
steps.description: Derived the vault key client-side from the master password and used it to unlock saved credentials locally. The master password is treated as the user's primary secret, not as a database value to be recovered by support.

steps.title: Added recovery with email plus recovery code
steps.description: Built the recovery path around two checks: access to the registered email account and possession of the recovery code. If the user loses both the master password and recovery code, the encrypted vault remains unrecoverable by design.

steps.title: Deployed the production lab
steps.description: Hosted the application on Vercel and connected it to Neon so the complete flow can be tested from the public URL, including sign-in, vault storage, and recovery behavior.

steps.title: Captured hardening improvements
steps.description: Marked the next lab checks as rate limiting for login and recovery, email-verification abuse protection, recovery-code rotation, database backup restore testing, CSP/security headers, audit logging, and a short threat model.

outcomes: Working cloud deployment available for review at https://firoz-passdesk.vercel.app/
Clear user-facing warning added around irreversible vault loss when both master password and recovery code are missing
Recovery model is stronger than email-only reset because the recovery code remains a required user-held factor
Good portfolio lab candidate because it shows security architecture, cloud deployment, database design, and user-risk communication
Remaining lab polish: publish a threat model, add screenshots, document crypto choices, and record backup/restore and rate-limit tests

title: Hardening an Apache Web Server with SELinux & firewalld
summary: Served a site on a non-standard port, resolved SELinux AVC denials without disabling enforcement, and locked the host down with firewalld zones and rich rules.
objectives: Serve a static site on TCP 8080 instead of the default port 80
Diagnose and fix SELinux denials caused by the non-standard port
Restrict inbound access using firewalld zones and a rich rule scoped to one subnet

steps.title: Install and enable Apache
steps.description: Installed httpd, started and enabled it so it survives a reboot, and confirmed the default page loads on port 80 first.

steps.title: Move the listener to port 8080
steps.description: Changed the Listen directive and restarted the service. The restart succeeded, but the socket never came up — journalctl showed httpd exiting with a permission error even though the file permissions were correct.

steps.title: Diagnose and fix the SELinux denial
steps.description: ausearch confirmed an AVC denial for httpd_t trying to bind to a port not labelled http_port_t. Rather than setenforce 0, I added 8080 to the correct SELinux port type and verified the policy with semanage.

steps.title: Lock down access with firewalld
steps.description: Opened 8080/tcp permanently, then added a rich rule so only the lab's management subnet can reach it — everything else is dropped by the default zone.
commands: firewall-cmd --permanent --add-rich-rule='rule family="ipv4" source address="10.0.2.0/24" port port="8080" protocol="tcp" accept'

outcomes: Site reachable on :8080 with SELinux still in enforcing mode
Confirmed the difference between a firewall block and an SELinux denial when troubleshooting a 'service won't start' issue
Wrote a one-page runbook for re-applying this config on a fresh VM

title: LVM Storage: Online Resize, Shrink & Snapshot Rollback
summary: Built a multi-disk LVM layout on RHEL, grew an XFS filesystem online, shrank an ext4 volume safely, and used a snapshot to roll back a bad change.
objectives: Create a volume group from two physical volumes and grow it across disks
Extend a live XFS filesystem without downtime
Take an LVM snapshot before a risky change and restore from it
Shrink an ext4 logical volume safely (XFS cannot be shrunk)

steps.title: Create physical volumes and a volume group
steps.description: Initialised two disks as PVs and combined them into a single volume group, then checked the free extents available.

steps.title: Create and mount an XFS logical volume
steps.description: Created a 6 GiB logical volume spanning both disks, formatted it XFS, and mounted it with an entry in fstab using its UUID.

steps.title: Grow the filesystem online
steps.description: Extended the logical volume by 2 GiB using the remaining free space, then grew the XFS filesystem in place — no unmount required.

steps.title: Snapshot before a risky change, then roll back
steps.description: Took a copy-on-write snapshot of a separate ext4 volume before editing config files on it. After intentionally breaking the config, I unmounted, reverted using lvconvert --merge, and remounted with the original data intact.

steps.title: Shrink an ext4 volume safely
steps.description: Because XFS can't shrink, this step used an ext4 volume: unmounted it, ran a filesystem check, shrank the filesystem first, then shrank the logical volume — never the other way around.

outcomes: Comfortable growing live filesystems without scheduling downtime
Internalised the 'shrink the filesystem before the LV, never after' rule
Snapshot + merge gave a real rollback path for config changes — documented as part of a change-management checklist

title: Replacing Cron with a systemd Service + Timer for Backups
summary: Wrote a Bash backup script, packaged it as a systemd service unit, and scheduled it with a systemd timer — with status, logs and failure handling all visible through systemctl and journalctl.
environment: Target: nightly backup of /etc and /srv to /backup
objectives: Write an idempotent backup script with clear exit codes
Run it as an unprivileged systemd service rather than a root cron job
Schedule it with a systemd timer, including a randomised delay
Make failures easy to spot via systemctl status and journald

steps.title: Write the backup script
steps.description: Created /usr/local/sbin/run-backup.sh — it tars /etc and /srv into a timestamped archive under /backup, prunes anything older than 14 days, and logs progress to stdout (captured by journald).

steps.title: Create the service unit
steps.description: Defined a oneshot service that runs the script as a dedicated 'backup' user, with Type=oneshot so systemd waits for it to finish before marking it done.

steps.title: Add the timer unit
steps.description: Created a matching .timer unit set to run daily at 02:00 with a 10-minute RandomizedDelaySec so multiple lab VMs don't all hit disk I/O at once. Enabled the timer, not the service, so it persists across reboots.

steps.title: Verify logs and failure handling
steps.description: Forced a failure (read-only /backup mount) to confirm the service reports 'failed', the timer still fires the next day, and journalctl shows a clear error without digging through log files.

outcomes: One command (systemctl status) shows whether last night's backup succeeded — no log-grepping required
RandomizedDelaySec and Persistent=true behaviour now make sense in production cron replacements
Reusable unit-file template for future scheduled jobs

title: Centralising Home Directories with NFS and autofs
summary: Exported user home directories from a RHEL NFS server and mounted them on-demand on a client with autofs, with SELinux booleans set correctly instead of disabling the policy.
objectives: Export /home/guests from the server, restricted to the client's subnet
Mount it on the client automatically on access using autofs, not a static fstab entry
Get SELinux booleans right so NFS home directories actually work

steps.title: Configure the NFS export
steps.description: Created the export directory, added an entry to /etc/exports restricted to the lab subnet with root_squash, and exported it.

steps.title: Open the firewall for NFS
steps.description: Used the predefined 'nfs' firewalld service rather than guessing ports, since NFS uses several auxiliary services (rpc-bind, mountd).

steps.title: Configure autofs on the client
steps.description: Added a master map entry pointing /home/guests at an indirect map, so home directories mount on first access and unmount after a period of inactivity — instead of mounting everything at boot.

steps.title: Fix the SELinux boolean
steps.description: The first login attempt failed with permission denied even though Unix permissions were fine. getsebool showed use_nfs_home_dirs was off — enabling it (persistently) fixed logins without touching enforcing mode.

outcomes: Same home directory follows a user between two client VMs
Learned to reach for setsebool -P before ever reaching for setenforce 0
autofs idle-unmount behaviour confirmed with automount -m

title: NIC Bonding and VLAN Tagging with nmcli
summary: Configured an active-backup bond across two virtual NICs and added 802.1Q VLAN sub-interfaces on top of it, entirely through nmcli and NetworkManager connection profiles.
objectives: Create an active-backup bond from two interfaces
Add tagged VLAN sub-interfaces (VLAN 10 and 20) on top of the bond
Verify failover by downing the active slave and confirming connectivity holds

steps.title: Create the bond and add slaves
steps.description: Created a bond0 connection in active-backup mode, then attached both physical interfaces as slaves and brought everything up.
commands: nmcli con add type bond ifname bond0 mode active-backup
nmcli con add type ethernet ifname enp0s8 master bond0
nmcli con add type ethernet ifname enp0s9 master bond0

steps.title: Assign a static IP to the bond
steps.description: Set a static address on bond0 rather than DHCP, matching the lab's static addressing scheme, and confirmed it came up.
commands: nmcli con mod bond0 ipv4.addresses 192.168.56.20/24 ipv4.method manual

steps.title: Add tagged VLAN sub-interfaces
steps.description: Created two VLAN interfaces on top of bond0 — VLAN 10 for a 'servers' segment and VLAN 20 for 'management' — each with its own subnet.
commands: nmcli con add type vlan con-name bond0.10 ifname bond0.10 dev bond0 id 10 ip4 10.0.10.5/24
nmcli con add type vlan con-name bond0.20 ifname bond0.20 dev bond0 id 20 ip4 10.0.20.5/24
nmcli con up bond0.10 && nmcli con up bond0.20

steps.title: Test failover
steps.description: Started a continuous ping over bond0.10, then set the active slave down with nmcli and watched cat /proc/net/bonding/bond0 to confirm the backup slave took over with no dropped pings.

outcomes: Bond + VLAN config survives a reboot — all stored as NetworkManager connection profiles, no manual ifcfg editing
Confirmed active-backup failover with zero packet loss in the lab
Comfortable explaining the difference between nmcli con and nmcli dev commands
